children.name: Taeniopygia
children.description: The zebra finches — a small genus of Australasian estrildids known for their distinctively patterned plumage and remarkable adaptability to arid environments. The Zebra Finch is one of the most studied songbirds in laboratory research, particularly in neurobiology, learning, and vocal communication. They are social, gregarious birds that form enormous flocks in suitable habitat.

children.name: Taeniopygia guttata
children.description: One of the most popular and well-studied cage birds in the world, the Zebra Finch is also abundant in the wild across most of Australia and the Lesser Sunda Islands. The male has a striking pattern of black-and-white 'zebra' stripes on the throat and chest, bright orange cheek patches, and a chestnut flank with white spots. It breeds opportunistically in response to rainfall, often raising multiple clutches in quick succession in the arid interior.

children.name: Padda
children.description: The Java sparrows — a small genus of stocky, large-billed estrildids native to islands of Southeast Asia. They are notable for their thick, conical bills adapted for husking rice and other grains. The Java Sparrow has been a popular cage bird in Asia for centuries, which has led to extensive introductions across the tropics. They are highly social and often roost in large communal groups.

children.name: Padda oryzivora
children.description: A striking, plump finch with a grey body, black head, white cheek patches, and a massive pink-red bill. It is native to Java, Bali, and Bawean in Indonesia but has been introduced widely across Asia, Africa, and the Pacific. Its fondness for rice earned it the scientific name oryzivora and made it a pest of agricultural importance, but its popularity as a cage bird has led to severe declines in its native range.

children.name: Padda fuscata
children.description: A plump, brownish finch endemic to Timor and nearby islands in the Lesser Sundas. It has a dark brown body, pale belly, and a thick pale bill, and inhabits grasslands and rice paddies.

children.name: Erythrura
children.description: The parrotfinches — a genus of brightly coloured estrildids with green, red, and blue plumage that give them a parrot-like appearance. They are found from Southeast Asia to the Pacific islands, with the highest diversity in New Guinea. Their stout, curved bills are adapted for feeding on grass seeds, figs, and bamboo seeds. Many species are prized in aviculture.

children.name: Erythrura gouldiae
children.description: One of the most dazzlingly coloured of all small passerines, the Gouldian Finch occurs in three naturally occurring colour morphs: red-headed, black-headed, and yellow-headed. It is endemic to northern Australia, where it inhabits tropical savanna and woodlands, feeding almost exclusively on sorghum and other grass seeds. Wild populations declined severely in the 20th century due to altered fire regimes and habitat loss, but captive breeding programmes have been extremely successful.

children.name: Erythrura psittacea
children.description: A stunning parrotfinch with a red face and throat, green body, and a red tail. It is endemic to New Caledonia, inhabiting forests and gardens, and is a popular aviary bird.

children.name: Erythrura pealii
children.description: A green parrotfinch with a red face and throat, blue belly, and red rump and tail. It is endemic to the Fijian archipelago, inhabiting forests and grasslands.

children.name: Erythrura kleinschmidti
children.description: A large parrotfinch with a distinctive pinkish-white bill, green body, black face, and red rump and tail. It is endemic to Fiji and is considered vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Erythrura prasina
children.description: A green parrotfinch with a red face, throat, and rump, and elongated central tail feathers. It ranges from Myanmar through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo, inhabiting forests and bamboo groves.

children.name: Erythrura hyperythra
children.description: A parrotfinch with a tawny breast, green upperparts, red rump and tail, and a blue face. It is found from Myanmar to the Philippines, Sulawesi, and the Lesser Sundas.

children.name: Erythrura tricolor
children.description: A small parrotfinch with a red head and rump, blue underparts, and green upperparts. It is endemic to Timor and the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Erythrura papuana
children.description: A green parrotfinch with a red face, blue belly, and red rump and tail, found throughout the mountains of New Guinea. It inhabits montane forests.

children.name: Erythrura regia
children.description: A brilliantly coloured parrotfinch with red head and body, blue belly and wings, and a green back. It is endemic to Vanuatu in the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Erythrura cyaneovirens
children.description: A parrotfinch with a red head, blue underparts, green upperparts, and a red rump. It is endemic to Samoa in the south Pacific.

children.name: Lonchura
children.description: The munias and mannikins — a large genus of seed-eating estrildids distributed across Africa, Asia, and Australasia. They are stout, typically brown or black seedeaters with conical bills, often with contrasting white or dark heads and bellies. Highly gregarious, they form noisy flocks in grasslands and rice paddies. Several species have been domesticated and are popular cage birds, including the Society Finch.

children.name: Lonchura domestica
children.description: A domesticated finch derived from the White-rumped Munia through centuries of selection in Japan and China. It is not found in the wild but exists only in captivity, where it is known for its gregarious, non-aggressive temperament and free-breeding habits. Its varied colour morphs include chocolate, pied, and fawn. It is often used as a foster parent for other estrildids in aviculture.

children.name: Lonchura punctulata
children.description: A small brown munia with a distinctive scaled pattern on the white breast and belly, giving it a snake-like appearance. It is native from India and Sri Lanka through Southeast Asia to the Philippines, Sulawesi, and northern Australia. It has been introduced to Hawaii, Puerto Rico, and parts of the Caribbean. It forms large, tight-knit flocks in rice paddies and grasslands, feeding heavily on grass seeds.

children.name: Lonchura malacca
children.description: A striking munia with a black head and breast, chestnut body, and white belly. It is found from India and Sri Lanka through Southeast Asia to Java and Bali.

children.name: Lonchura atricapilla
children.description: A munia with a black head, chestnut back and wings, and black belly. It ranges from Nepal and India through southern China to Taiwan, the Philippines, and Borneo.

children.name: Lonchura ferruginosa
children.description: A munia with a white crown and nape, chestnut body, and black face and belly. It is endemic to Java and Bali in Indonesia.

children.name: Lonchura molucca
children.description: A munia with a black face and throat, brown upperparts, and white underparts with black barring on the flanks. It is found in the Moluccas and Sulawesi region of Indonesia.

children.name: Lonchura caniceps
children.description: A munia with a grey head and breast, brown back, black belly, and yellow rump. It is endemic to Papua New Guinea, inhabiting grasslands and savanna.

children.name: Lonchura vana
children.description: A rare munia with a grey breast band, brown upperparts, white belly, and black head. It is endemic to the Arfak Mountains of western New Guinea.

children.name: Lonchura grandis
children.description: A large munia with a black head and breast, chestnut upperparts, and white belly with black barring. It is found in lowland grasslands of Papua New Guinea.

children.name: Lonchura bicolor
children.description: A small munia with a black head, back, and breast, white belly and flanks, and chestnut rump. It is widespread across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna and woodland.

children.name: Lonchura nigriceps
children.description: A munia with a black head, chestnut upperparts, white underparts, and chestnut flanks. It is found in savanna and grasslands from Somalia to South Africa.

children.name: Lonchura castaneothorax
children.description: A colourful munia with a chestnut breast band, black head, grey crown, and yellow belly. It is found in northern and eastern Australia, New Guinea, and the Lesser Sundas.

children.name: Lonchura leucogastra
children.description: A small munia with a white belly, brown upperparts, and a dark brown head and breast. It ranges from Myanmar through Thailand and Malaysia to Sumatra, Borneo, and the Philippines.

children.name: Lonchura quinticolor
children.description: A colourful munia with a chestnut face and throat, blue-grey crown, brown back, black belly, and chestnut flanks. It is endemic to the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Lonchura striata
children.description: A small, dark munia with a white rump, finely barred breast, and pointed tail. It ranges from India through southern China to Southeast Asia, and is the wild ancestor of the Society Finch.

children.name: Lonchura kelaarti
children.description: A munia with a black throat, chestnut upperparts, white underparts, and black belly. It is found in Sri Lanka and the Western Ghats of India.

children.name: Lonchura maja
children.description: A distinctive munia with a white head and throat, chestnut body, and white belly. It is found from Malaysia through Sumatra and Java to Bali.

children.name: Lonchura pallida
children.description: A pale munia with a buff-white head and breast, brown upperparts, and white belly. It is endemic to the islands of Flores and Sumbawa in the Lesser Sundas.

children.name: Lonchura stygia
children.description: A completely black munia with a pale blue-grey bill, endemic to the lowland swamps of southern New Guinea. It is considered near threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Lonchura teerinki
children.description: A munia with a black face and throat, brown upperparts, white belly, and chestnut flanks. It is endemic to the mountains of western New Guinea.

children.name: Lonchura montana
children.description: A high-altitude munia with a black face and throat, brown upperparts, white belly, and dark flanks. It is endemic to the Snow Mountains of western New Guinea.

children.name: Lonchura spectabilis
children.description: A striking munia with a black hood, chestnut back, white belly, and bright yellow rump. It is found in New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Lonchura nevermanni
children.description: A munia with a grey crown, black face and throat, chestnut upperparts, white belly, and chestnut flanks. It is found in lowland grasslands of southern New Guinea.

children.name: Lonchura hunsteini
children.description: A dark munia with blackish upperparts mottled with white, and white underparts with black spotting. It is endemic to New Ireland in the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Lonchura forbesi
children.description: A munia with a black head and breast, chestnut upperparts, and white belly with chestnut flanks. It is endemic to New Hanover and New Ireland in the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Lonchura nigerrima
children.description: A small blackish munia with a brown back, found in the lowlands of New Guinea and nearby islands.

children.name: Lonchura flaviprymna
children.description: A munia with a grey head, chestnut body, bright yellow rump, and white belly. It is endemic to northern Australia, inhabiting floodplain grasslands.

children.name: Lonchura monticola
children.description: A high-altitude munia with a black face and throat, brown upperparts, white belly, and dark flanks. It is endemic to the mountains of eastern New Guinea.

children.name: Lonchura mela
children.description: A small munia with black wings, brown body, and white belly. It is found in grasslands of the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Lonchura pectoralis
children.description: A munia with a black face and bib, white breast with dark scaling, brown back, and black belly. It is found in dry grasslands of northern Australia.

children.name: Lonchura cucullata
children.description: A small munia with a bronze-green gloss on the black head and breast, brown back, and white belly. It is found in savannas of West and Central Africa.

children.name: Lonchura fringilloides
children.description: A large munia with a black head and breast, brown back, and white belly and wings. It is found in savannas of eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Amadina
children.description: The cut-throat and related finches — a small genus of African estrildids with stout cones and distinctive throat patterns. The Cut-throat Finch is named for the male's vivid red throat band that resembles a wound. These are birds of dry savannas and scrublands, often gathering at waterholes in large numbers. They build untidy grass nests in trees or bushes, often reusing old weaver nests.

children.name: Amadina fasciata
children.description: A distinctive finch of dry savannas across sub-Saharan Africa, the male sports a brilliant crimson band across the throat that looks like a bloody cut. The rest of the plumage is brown with fine barring on the upperparts and a finely spotted breast. It is a common bird of thorn scrub and grassy woodlands, often seen in flocks at waterholes, and is a popular aviary bird worldwide.

children.name: Amadina erythrocephala
children.description: A finch with a red head and breast in the male, brown upperparts with fine barring, and white belly. It is found in savanna and scrublands of southern Africa.

children.name: Amandava
children.description: The avadavats — a small genus of brilliantly plumaged Asian estrildids with striking red breeding plumage in males. They are among the most colourful small passerines in Asia, with the male Red Avadavat covered in tiny white spots set against a red background. They frequent tall grasslands and wetlands, building domed grass nests with a side entrance.

children.name: Amandava amandava
children.description: A tiny, brilliantly coloured finch in which the breeding male is crimson-red covered with small white spots, with red wings and tail. It is found across the Indian subcontinent to Southeast Asia, inhabiting grasslands, reedbeds, and rice paddies. Its high-pitched, tinkling song and undulating flight make it a noticeable presence in wet grasslands, and it is a popular cage bird in Asia.

children.name: Amandava formosa
children.description: A beautiful green-and-yellow finch with fine white spotting on the wings and flanks, and a red bill. It is endemic to central India, inhabiting dry grasslands and scrub. It is considered vulnerable due to trapping for the cage bird trade.

children.name: Amandava subflava
children.description: A tiny finch with bright yellow underparts, an orange breast band in the male, olive-green upperparts, and a red rump. It is found across sub-Saharan Africa in moist grasslands and marshes.

children.name: Uraeginthus
children.description: The blue waxbills — a small genus of strikingly coloured African estrildids with brilliant blue faces, throats, and underparts. These delicate, fine-billed seedeaters are among the most beautiful African finches, occurring across savanna and woodland habitats. They are active, cheerful birds, often seen in pairs or small flocks at the edges of woodlands and in gardens.

children.name: Uraeginthus angolensis
children.description: A delightful little finch with a sky-blue face, throat, and breast contrasting with a pale belly and brownish upperparts. It is common across southern and central Africa in savanna, woodland edges, and gardens, often visiting waterholes in small parties. Its soft, high-pitched twittering calls and active, acrobatic movements make it a favourite among garden birdwatchers in South Africa.

children.name: Uraeginthus bengalus
children.description: A beautiful waxbill with sky-blue face and underparts, a brown back, and a red cheek patch in the male. It is widespread across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna and scrub habitats. Pairs are often seen at waterholes, and its soft, high-pitched calls and confiding nature make it a popular aviary bird.

children.name: Uraeginthus cyanocephalus
children.description: A striking waxbill with a blue crown and nape, blue breast and belly, and a warm brown back. It is found in East Africa from Somalia and Ethiopia to Tanzania, inhabiting dry savanna and thorn scrub. It is often seen in small flocks foraging on the ground for grass seeds.

children.name: Estrilda
children.description: The typical waxbills — a large genus of small, delicately proportioned estrildids found across Africa. They are named for the waxy red colouration of their bills, which resembles sealing wax. These active, social finches are predominantly fawn, grey, and brown with fine barring, and many show a distinctive red or black eye-stripe. They feed on grass seeds and small insects, often in large flocks at water sources.

children.name: Estrilda astrild
children.description: The common waxbill, also known as the St Helena waxbill, is a small passerine bird belonging to the estrildid finch family. It is native to sub-Saharan Africa but has been introduced to many other regions of the world and now has an estimated global extent of occurrence of 10,000,000 km2. It is popular and easy to keep in captivity.

children.name: Estrilda melpoda
children.description: A tiny, lively waxbill with a bright orange cheek patch, a red rump, and a pinkish-red bill. It ranges across West and Central Africa from Senegal to Angola and east to Uganda, inhabiting rank grasslands and marshy areas. It is a popular aviary bird and has been introduced to Puerto Rico, Bermuda, and parts of Japan, where small feral populations persist.

children.name: Estrilda troglodytes
children.description: A tiny waxbill of the Sahel and East African savannas, with pale fawn upperparts, a black rump, a red eye-stripe, and a pinkish-red bill. It is common in dry grasslands and thorn scrub, often in large flocks. It has been introduced to Hawaii and parts of Europe.

children.name: Estrilda rhodopyga
children.description: A small, slender waxbill with a bright crimson rump and upper tail, grey-brown upperparts, and a red eye-stripe. Found in East Africa from Sudan to Tanzania, inhabiting moist grasslands and marshes. It is often seen in small flocks, its crimson rump flashing conspicuously in flight.

children.name: Estrilda paludicola
children.description: A pale, fawn-coloured waxbill with a red rump, red eye-stripe, and a greyish breast. It is widespread in wetlands and moist savannas across Central and East Africa. Gregarious and active, it forms small flocks that feed on grass seeds in marshy areas.

children.name: Estrilda nonnula
children.description: A waxbill with a black crown and nape, grey-brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a red rump. It is found in Central and East Africa, inhabiting moist grasslands and forest edges.

children.name: Estrilda atricapilla
children.description: A waxbill with a black head, grey upperparts, white underparts, and a red rump. It is found in the lowland forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Estrilda kandti
children.description: A small waxbill with grey-brown upperparts, a red rump, pale underparts with a pinkish wash, and a red eye-stripe. It is found in the highlands of the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, Rwanda, and Uganda.

children.name: Estrilda poliopareia
children.description: A waxbill with brown upperparts, a grey face and breast, a red rump, and a red eye-stripe. It is endemic to the Anambra region of Nigeria, inhabiting reed beds and grasslands near the Niger River.

children.name: Estrilda charmosyna
children.description: A waxbill with a red face and rump, grey-brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a black belly. It is found in dry savannas of East Africa from Somalia to Tanzania.

children.name: Estrilda nigriloris
children.description: A waxbill with a distinctive black stripe through the eye, grey-brown upperparts, red rump, and pale underparts. It is endemic to the highlands of southern Tanzania.

children.name: Brunhilda
children.description: A small genus of African waxbills with distinctive black, white, and red facial patterns. They were formerly included in Estrilda but are distinguished by their heavier bills and striking black-and-red facial masks. They inhabit dry savanna and thorn scrub across eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Brunhilda erythronotos
children.description: A striking waxbill with a black face and cheek patch, a red rump and belly, and fine barring on the upperparts. Found in dry savanna and thorn scrub from Angola and Namibia to Kenya. It is often seen in pairs or small parties, and its active, twittering behaviour makes it a conspicuous presence in arid landscapes.

children.name: Spermophaga
children.description: The bluebills — a small genus of large, robust estrildids with heavy blue-grey bills adapted for cracking hard seeds. They are found in forests and dense thickets across tropical Africa. Males are strikingly coloured with red, black, and white plumage, while females are duller. They are shy, retiring birds that forage on the forest floor.

children.name: Spermophaga ruficapilla
children.description: A large, striking finch with a bright red head and breast, black body, and a massive blue-grey bill. Found in Central and East African forests from Nigeria to Kenya and Tanzania. It is shy and secretive, foraging on the forest floor for fallen seeds and small fruits.

children.name: Spermophaga poliogenys
children.description: A large bluebill with a red breast and belly, black upperparts, and a massive blue-grey bill. It is found in lowland forests of Central Africa from Nigeria to Uganda.

children.name: Spermophaga haematina
children.description: A robust finch with a red breast and belly in the male, black upperparts, and a massive blue-grey bill. Found in lowland forests of West Africa from Senegal to Cameroon.

children.name: Mandingoa
children.description: A monotypic genus of African estrildid containing the Green Twinspot. It is a beautifully coloured finch of lowland and montane forests, with a glittering spotted breast that gives it its name. Its iridescent green spots are unique among African waxbills.

children.name: Mandingoa nitidula
children.description: A stunning little finch with a red face and throat, olive-green upperparts covered in iridescent green spots, and a black belly. Found in forests from Sierra Leone to Ethiopia and south to Zimbabwe and Mozambique. It is shy and difficult to observe, foraging quietly in the undergrowth for small seeds.

children.name: Lagonosticta
children.description: The firefinches — a genus of small, beautifully coloured African estrildids named for the brilliant crimson and pink plumage of the males. They are among the most vividly coloured small birds in Africa, with females being duller brownish versions. They inhabit savanna, scrub, and forest edges, often near water, and build domed grass nests with a side entrance.

children.name: Lagonosticta senegala
children.description: A tiny, brilliantly coloured finch with a red bill, red face and breast, and brown upperparts. The male is a vivid crimson-red; the female is brown with a red bill and rump. It is one of the most widespread firefinches, found across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna, gardens, and cultivated areas.

children.name: Lagonosticta rubricata
children.description: A striking firefinch with dark crimson upperparts, a red face and breast, and fine white spots on the flanks. Found from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to South Africa, inhabiting moist savanna, forest edges, and rank grasslands. It is shyer than the Red-billed Firefinch and prefers denser cover.

children.name: Lagonosticta rhodopareia
children.description: A small firefinch with a pinkish-red face and breast, brown upperparts, and a red rump. Found in East and southern Africa from Ethiopia to South Africa. It inhabits dry savanna, thorn scrub, and grassland, often near termite mounds, and is regularly seen in small family parties.

children.name: Lagonosticta rufopicta
children.description: A firefinch with a red face and breast, brown upperparts, and distinctive white barring on the breast. It is found across West and Central Africa in moist savanna and grassland.

children.name: Lagonosticta nitidula
children.description: A duller firefinch with brown upperparts, pinkish-red face and breast, and a dark bill. It is found in Central and East Africa in moist grasslands and marshes.

children.name: Lagonosticta landanae
children.description: A firefinch with a red face and breast, grey-brown upperparts, and a pinkish belly. It is found in the coastal region of the Cabinda exclave of Angola and adjacent Democratic Republic of the Congo.

children.name: Lagonosticta virata
children.description: A firefinch with a bright red face and breast, brown upperparts, and a pinkish belly. It is endemic to the dry savannas of Mali and Senegal in West Africa.

children.name: Lagonosticta sanguinodorsalis
children.description: A firefinch with a red face, throat, and rump, grey-brown upperparts, and a pinkish belly. It is endemic to the Jos Plateau of Nigeria, inhabiting rocky outcrops and dry savanna.

children.name: Lagonosticta umbrinodorsalis
children.description: A firefinch with a red face and breast, dark brown upperparts, and a pinkish belly. It is found in the dry savannas of Chad and Cameroon.

children.name: Granatina
children.description: A small genus of spectacular African estrildids noted for their brilliant violet, blue, and chestnut plumage. The Violet-eared Waxbill and Purple Grenadier are among the most beautiful of all African finches, with males displaying an extraordinary array of iridescent colours. They inhabit dry savanna and thorn scrub.

children.name: Granatina granatina
children.description: A stunning finch with a violet face and ear coverts, a chestnut crown and back, a blue throat and belly, and a red bill. Found in savanna and scrub from Angola and Zambia to South Africa. It is often seen in pairs, and its beauty makes it highly sought-after by birdwatchers visiting southern Africa.

children.name: Granatina ianthinogaster
children.description: A spectacular finch with a chestnut back, a brilliant violet-blue belly and rump, a red bill, and a long graduated tail. Found in East Africa from Somalia and Ethiopia to Tanzania. The male is one of the most vividly coloured of all estrildids, with the violet belly flashing conspicuously in flight.

children.name: Coccopygia
children.description: A small genus of African waxbills with yellow or greenish underparts and distinctive red rumps. They are among the smallest estrildids, with delicate bills and active, acrobatic feeding habits. They inhabit montane grasslands, forest edges, and cultivated areas in eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Coccopygia quartinia
children.description: A tiny waxbill with olive-green upperparts, bright yellow underparts, a red rump, and a black bill. Found in East African highlands from Ethiopia to Tanzania and Malawi. Inhabits montane grasslands and forest edges, often in small flocks feeding on grass seeds.

children.name: Coccopygia melanotis
children.description: A tiny waxbill with a grey crown, olive back, yellow belly, red rump, and black ear patch. It is endemic to southern Africa, found in montane grasslands and coastal scrub from Zimbabwe to the Cape. Its high-pitched, sibilant 'swee-swee' call gives it its common name.

children.name: Pyrenestes
children.description: The seedcrackers — a small genus of African estrildids with massively thick, powerful bills adapted for cracking the hardest seeds. They exhibit a remarkable polymorphism in bill size, with two distinct morphs occurring within the same population. They inhabit forests and dense thickets.

children.name: Pyrenestes sanguineus
children.description: A finch with a massive black bill, crimson red head and breast, black belly, and brown back. It is found in forests of West Africa from Senegal to Nigeria.

children.name: Pyrenestes ostrinus
children.description: A seedcracker with an enormous bill, crimson head and breast, black belly, and brown back. It is found in Central African forests from Cameroon to Uganda and south to Angola.

children.name: Pyrenestes minor
children.description: A smaller seedcracker with a large black bill, red face and breast, black belly, and olive-brown back. It is endemic to the forests of Tanzania and Malawi.

children.name: Pytilia
children.description: The pytilias — a genus of colourful African estrildids with green or red wings and finely barred underparts. They are active, often confiding birds of savanna and woodland edges. Their flight is fast and direct with a characteristic whirring sound.

children.name: Pytilia melba
children.description: A colourful finch with a red face and throat, grey crown, green wings, and finely barred white underparts. It is widespread across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna and thorn scrub.

children.name: Pytilia afra
children.description: A finch with a red face and breast, olive-green upperparts, bright orange wing patches, and finely barred grey underparts. It is found in East and southern Africa.

children.name: Pytilia phoenicoptera
children.description: A pytilia with a grey head and breast, olive upperparts, bright red wings, and finely barred underparts. It is found in West Africa from Senegal to Nigeria.

children.name: Pytilia hypogrammica
children.description: A pytilia with a red face, olive upperparts, yellow wing patches, and finely barred underparts. It is found in Central Africa from Nigeria to Uganda.

children.name: Cryptospiza
children.description: The crimson-wings — a small genus of shy, forest-dwelling African estrildids with red wings, olive-green bodies, and dark faces. They are secretive birds of montane forest undergrowth, often difficult to observe as they forage quietly in deep shade.

children.name: Cryptospiza shelleyi
children.description: A beautiful finch with a crimson face and wings, olive-green crown and back, and grey underparts. It is found in montane forests of the Albertine Rift in Central Africa.

children.name: Cryptospiza jacksoni
children.description: A dusky finch with crimson wings, olive-green upperparts, and dark grey underparts. It is found in montane forests of the Albertine Rift.

children.name: Cryptospiza salvadorii
children.description: A finch with crimson wings, dark brown upperparts, and grey underparts. It is found in montane forests of East Africa from Ethiopia to Tanzania.

children.name: Cryptospiza reichenovii
children.description: A finch with a red face and wings, olive-green upperparts, and grey underparts. It is found in lowland and montane forests of Central and East Africa.

children.name: Hypargos
children.description: The twinspots — a small genus of beautifully patterned African estrildids with spotted breasts and bright rumps. They are shy, retiring birds of forest undergrowth and dense thickets, often more easily heard than seen. Their melodious calls are characteristic sounds of African forests.

children.name: Hypargos niveoguttatus
children.description: A striking finch with a red throat and breast spotted with white, olive-brown upperparts, a red rump, and a black belly. It is found in forests and dense thickets of Central and East Africa.

children.name: Hypargos margaritatus
children.description: A beautiful finch with a pink throat and breast covered in white spots, olive-brown upperparts, red rump, and black belly. It is endemic to the coastal forests of southern Mozambique and South Africa.

children.name: Euschistospiza
children.description: A small genus of African estrildids related to the twinspots, with finely spotted breasts and rich maroon or dusky plumage. They are shy, secretive birds of montane and lowland forests, foraging on the ground in dense cover.

children.name: Euschistospiza dybowskii
children.description: A finch with a maroon-red face and breast spotted with white, dark grey upperparts, and a black belly. It is found in forests of Central Africa from Nigeria to Uganda.

children.name: Euschistospiza cinereovinacea
children.description: A dusky finch with a vinaceous-red breast spotted with white, dark grey upperparts, and a black belly. It is found in the highlands of the Albertine Rift in Central Africa.

children.name: Nigrita
children.description: The negrofinches — a genus of African estrildids with contrasting black, grey, and white plumage, found in forests and woodlands across sub-Saharan Africa. They are active, acrobatic birds that forage in the canopy and understory, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Nigrita bicolor
children.description: A finch with a chestnut breast and belly, black cap, grey back, and white rump. It is found in forests and woodlands of West and Central Africa.

children.name: Nigrita luteifrons
children.description: A small finch with a pale yellow forehead, grey crown, olive-green upperparts, and grey underparts. It is found in lowland forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Nigrita canicapilla
children.description: A finch with a grey head and breast, black upperparts, and white belly. It is widespread in forests and woodlands across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Nigrita fusconota
children.description: A finch with a black head and upperparts, white breast and belly, and white rump. It is found in lowland forests of Central and West Africa.

children.name: Nesocharis
children.description: The olivebacks — a small genus of African estrildids with olive-green and grey plumage, thick bills, and a distinctive white collar. They are found in forests and montane scrub of West and Central Africa, where they forage quietly in the canopy for seeds and small fruits.

children.name: Nesocharis ansorgei
children.description: A finch with a grey head and breast, white collar, olive-green back, and yellow belly. It is found in montane forests of the Albertine Rift in Central Africa.

children.name: Nesocharis shelleyi
children.description: A beautiful finch with a grey head, white collar, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. It is found in montane forests of Cameroon and Nigeria.

children.name: Nesocharis capistrata
children.description: A finch with a grey head, white throat, olive-green upperparts, and yellow belly. It is found in lowland forests of West Africa from Guinea to Nigeria.

children.name: Parmoptila
children.description: The antpeckers — a small genus of African estrildids with finely spotted plumage and a unique association with ants. They are named for their habit of following ant columns to feed on insects flushed by the ants, a behaviour unusual among estrildids. They have stout, rather finch-like bills.

children.name: Parmoptila rubrifrons
children.description: A small finch with a red forehead, brown upperparts finely spotted with white, and pale underparts. It is found in lowland forests of West Africa.

children.name: Parmoptila jamesoni
children.description: A finch with a red forehead, olive-brown upperparts spotted with white, and pale underparts with fine spotting. It is found in forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Parmoptila woodhousei
children.description: A finch with a red forehead, dark brown upperparts with white spotting, and pale underparts. It is found in lowland forests of West and Central Africa.

children.name: Paludipasser
children.description: A monotypic genus of African estrildid containing the Locust Finch, a small, secretive bird of wetlands and moist grasslands. It is named for its preference for grasshopper and locust nymphs, which supplement its seed-based diet. It is one of the most elusive of the African waxbills.

children.name: Paludipasser locustellus
children.description: A tiny finch with brown upperparts, a red face and rump, and pale underparts. It is found in moist grasslands and marshes of Central and East Africa, often near water.

children.name: Ortygospiza
children.description: The quailfinches — a small genus of terrestrial estrildids resembling miniature quail in their shape and ground-dwelling habits. They are found in moist grasslands and marshes across Africa, where they forage on the ground for grass seeds. Their short, rounded wings and strong legs reflect their largely terrestrial lifestyle.

children.name: Ortygospiza atricollis
children.description: A tiny terrestrial finch with a black face and throat, brown upperparts finely barred with white, and pale underparts. It is found in moist grasslands across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Ortygospiza fuscocrissa
children.description: A small terrestrial finch with a black face, brown upperparts, and dark brown underparts with a white belly. It is found in moist grasslands of East and southern Africa.

children.name: Ortygospiza gabonensis
children.description: A tiny finch with a red bill, brown upperparts barred with white, and pale underparts. It is found in moist grasslands of Central Africa.

children.name: Amurestes
children.description: A genus of African estrildids with distinctive black, red, and white plumage patterns. The Crimson Seedcracker and related species have massively developed bills for cracking hard seeds.

children.name: Amurestes obscurus
children.description: A finch with a red face, dark brown upperparts, and grey underparts. It is found in forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Sporaeginthus
children.description: A small genus of African estrildids known as the seedeaters or cordon-bleus, with bright blue plumage on the face and underparts. They are among the most beautiful of the small African finches.

children.name: Sporaeginthus subflavus
children.description: A tiny waxbill with orange underparts in breeding males, olive-brown upperparts, and a red rump. It is found in moist grasslands across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Emblema
children.description: A monotypic genus of Australian estrildid containing the Painted Finch, a strikingly coloured bird of rocky hillsides and arid scrublands. It is named for its brilliant red and black facial pattern that resembles painted markings.

children.name: Emblema pictum
children.description: A striking finch with a red face and breast, black belly, white-spotted black flanks, and olive-brown back. It is endemic to the rocky ranges of arid and semi-arid Australia.

children.name: Stagonopleura
children.description: The firetails — a genus of Australian estrildids with brilliant crimson rumps and tails, found in coastal and forested regions of Australia. They are plump, active finches with distinctive black-and-white patterns on the face and throat.

children.name: Stagonopleura guttata
children.description: A beautiful finch with a bright red rump and tail, white underparts with a black breast band, white-spotted black flanks, and a red bill. It is found in woodlands and grasslands of eastern and southern Australia.

children.name: Stagonopleura bella
children.description: A finch with a bright red rump and tail, olive-brown upperparts finely barred with black, white underparts spotted with black, and a red bill. It is found in coastal heath and forests of southeastern Australia and Tasmania.

children.name: Stagonopleura oculata
children.description: A finch with a red ear patch and rump, olive upperparts, white underparts with black spotting, and a red bill. It is endemic to the forests of southwestern Australia.

children.name: Neochmia
children.description: A genus of Australian estrildids with brilliant red markings on the face and rump, found in grasslands and woodlands across Australia. They are lively, active finches that often gather in large flocks.

children.name: Neochmia temporalis
children.description: A small finch with a bright red eyebrow and rump, olive-green upperparts, grey underparts, and a red bill. It is common across eastern Australia in forests, woodlands, and gardens.

children.name: Neochmia modesta
children.description: A finch with a purple-red crown and face, red rump, olive-brown upperparts, and white underparts with fine barring. It is found in grasslands and woodlands of eastern Australia.

children.name: Neochmia ruficauda
children.description: A finch with a red face and forehead, bright yellow underparts with white spots, olive upperparts, and a red rump. It is found in grasslands and wetlands of northern and eastern Australia.

children.name: Poephila
children.description: The grassfinches — a genus of long-tailed Australian estrildids with black bibs and chestnut or grey plumage. They are birds of arid and semi-arid grasslands, often seen in large flocks drinking at waterholes.

children.name: Poephila acuticauda
children.description: A slender finch with a long pointed tail, fawn-brown upperparts, white underparts, black bib, and a yellow or red bill depending on subspecies. It is found in dry grasslands of northern Australia.

children.name: Poephila cincta
children.description: A finch with a black throat and bib, fawn-brown upperparts, white underparts, black rump, and a black pointed tail. It is found in grassy woodlands of eastern Australia.

children.name: Poephila personata
children.description: A finch with a black face mask, pale brown upperparts, white underparts, a thick yellow bill, and a pointed black tail. It is found in dry grasslands and savannas of northern Australia.

children.name: Heteromunia
children.description: A monotypic genus of Australian estrildid containing the Pictorella Munia, a distinctive finch with a scaly white breast and a blue-grey bill. It is found in dry grasslands of northern Australia.

children.name: Heteromunia pectoralis
children.description: A small finch with a blue-grey bill, a black face and bib, white breast with dark scaling, brown upperparts, and white belly. It is found in dry grasslands of northern Australia.

children.name: Odontospiza
children.description: A monotypic genus of African estrildid containing the Crimson-rumped Waxbill, a small finch with a bright red rump and distinctive facial markings. It is found in dry savannas of East Africa.

children.name: Odontospiza griseicapilla
children.description: A tiny waxbill with a grey crown, brown back, red rump, pale underparts, and a red eye-stripe. It is found in dry savannas of East Africa.

children.name: Glaucopectus
children.description: A genus of African estrildids with distinctive blue-grey bills and finely patterned plumage, found in savannas and woodlands of Africa.

children.name: Glaucopectus angolensis
children.description: A small waxbill with a blue-grey bill, brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a red rump. It is found in savannas of southwestern Africa.

children.name: Estrildopas
children.description: A small genus of African estrildids with black, white, and rufous plumage patterns, found in forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Estrildopas ruficeps
children.description: A waxbill with a rufous crown, black face, olive upperparts, and pale underparts. It is found in lowland forests of Central Africa.

children.name: Madagascara
children.description: A genus of estrildids endemic to Madagascar, with olive, yellow, and red plumage. They inhabit forests and scrub across the island.

children.name: Madagascara madagascariensis
children.description: A small finch with brown upperparts, white underparts, and a black face and throat. It is endemic to Madagascar, inhabiting grasslands and forests.

children.name: Madagascara nana
children.description: A tiny finch with brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a short tail. It is endemic to Madagascar, inhabiting grasslands and wetlands.

children.name: Carpophaga
children.description: A genus of Pacific island estrildids with thick bills and brown, black, and white plumage. They are found on islands of the southwest Pacific.

children.name: Carpophaga melanura
children.description: A stocky munia with a black tail, brown upperparts, white underparts, and a thick grey bill. It is found on islands of Fiji and Tonga.

children.name: Carpophaga pectoralis
children.description: A munia with a white breast, brown upperparts, black belly, and thick bill. It is found on islands of Vanuatu and New Caledonia.

children.name: Leucosticte
children.description: A genus of mountain-dwelling finches with grey, brown, and pink plumage, found in high-altitude regions of Asia and North America. They are adapted to the harshest environments on Earth.

children.name: Leucosticte nemoricola
children.description: A streaked brown finch with a pale face and stout bill. It is found in the Himalayas and Tibetan Plateau.

children.name: Leucosticte brandti
children.description: A grey-brown finch with a pink belly and wing patches. It is found in the high mountains of central Asia.

children.name: Leucosticte arctoa
children.description: A dark brown finch with pink wings and belly. It is found in the mountains of Siberia and northern China.

children.name: Calliphthalmus
children.description: A genus of African estrildids with bright red or orange bills and colourful plumage, found in savannas and woodlands.

children.name: Calliphthalmus erythrothorax
children.description: A waxbill with a red breast, brown upperparts, and a red bill. It is found in savannas of West Africa.

children.name: Calliphthalmus rubriventris
children.description: A waxbill with a red belly, brown upperparts, and a red bill. It is found in savannas of central Africa.

children.name: Calliphthalmus flaviventris
children.description: A waxbill with a yellow belly, brown upperparts, and a red bill. It is found in savannas of southern Africa.

children.name: Calliphthalmus nigrimentum
children.description: A waxbill with a black chin, brown upperparts, and pale underparts. It is found in savannas of East Africa.

children.name: Entomophaga
children.description: A genus of insectivorous estrildids found in the forests of Central Africa, with olive and yellow plumage and fine bills adapted for catching small insects.

children.name: Entomophaga insectivora
children.description: A small waxbill with olive upperparts, yellow underparts, and a fine black bill. It is found in lowland forests of central Africa.

children.name: Entomophaga chloropoda
children.description: A waxbill with green legs, olive upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is found in montane forests of East Africa.

children.name: Entomophaga flaviventris
children.description: A small finch with bright yellow underparts, olive upperparts, and a thin bill. It is found in forests of central Africa.
